BIRDS_IN_FLIGHT.rationale: 1/2000s freezes wing tip motion. Continuous AF with subject tracking keeps fast-moving birds in focus.
BIRDS_IN_FLIGHT.proTip: Pre-focus on a perched bird, then switch to tracking mode when it takes flight to maintain AF lock.
BIRDS_IN_FLIGHT.commonMistake: Using single-shot AF loses focus the instant the bird moves—always use continuous tracking AF.

LARGE_MAMMAL_RUNNING.rationale: 1/1000s freezes galloping gait. f/5.6 provides enough depth to keep the whole animal sharp.
LARGE_MAMMAL_RUNNING.proTip: Pan with the animal as it runs—this keeps the animal sharp while blurring the background for a sense of speed.
LARGE_MAMMAL_RUNNING.commonMistake: Stopping the pan at the moment of exposure creates blur—follow through as if throwing a bowling ball.

STATIC_WILDLIFE.rationale: Low ISO + f/8 for maximum detail on perched or stationary animals. Fast enough shutter for any unexpected movement.
STATIC_WILDLIFE.proTip: Get to eye level with your subject—looking down on wildlife creates a domineering perspective that feels unnatural.
STATIC_WILDLIFE.commonMistake: Shooting from standing height makes everything look small and unimpressive. Get low!

LOW_LIGHT_WILDLIFE.rationale: Dawn and dusk are peak activity times for wildlife. ISO 1600 is acceptable noise for the reward of behavioral shots.
LOW_LIGHT_WILDLIFE.proTip: Modern high-ISO performance is excellent—don't fear ISO 3200 if it means getting the shot at a critical moment.
LOW_LIGHT_WILDLIFE.commonMistake: Refusing to raise ISO beyond 800 means missing peak activity during golden hour light.

SMALL_BIRD_OVERCAST.rationale: Small birds move erratically at high speed. 1/3200 freezes wing beats. High ISO compensates for overcast conditions.
SMALL_BIRD_OVERCAST.proTip: Use a 600mm+ telephoto to maintain working distance without disturbing the bird's natural behavior.
SMALL_BIRD_OVERCAST.commonMistake: Getting too close to small birds causes them to flush—maintain distance and let the telephoto do the work.

PANNING_CREATIVE.rationale: Slow shutter panned with subject motion. f/16 and ND filter prevent overexposure in daylight at 1/30.
PANNING_CREATIVE.proTip: Practice on predictable subjects first—racing cars work better than wildlife for learning panning technique.
PANNING_CREATIVE.commonMistake: Varying the pan speed during exposure causes uneven motion blur that looks like a mistake.

SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.rationale: Expose for the bright sky. f/8 and fast shutter freeze animal outline perfectly against the burning sky.
SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.proTip: Know animal silhouettes—animals with distinctive shapes (giraffes, elephants, birds with spread wings) work best.
SILHOUETTE_SUNSET.commonMistake: Choosing animals with unclear silhouettes creates confusing images where the subject isn't identifiable.

SHADE_STATIC.rationale: Shaded animals require elevated ISO. Eye detection AF locks accurately on animal eyes for intimate portraits.
SHADE_STATIC.proTip: Animals in shade often display natural behavior—hunting, grooming, feeding. Patience rewards with authentic shots.
SHADE_STATIC.commonMistake: Metering for the bright background underexposes the subject—spot meter off the animal's body.

BLACK_AND_WHITE_WILDLIFE.rationale: Monochrome emphasizes texture, form, and emotion over color. Works perfectly for dramatic animal portraits.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_WILDLIFE.proTip: Strong sidelight or backlight creates better tonal separation in B&W than flat frontal light.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_WILDLIFE.commonMistake: Choosing subjects without strong texture or form—smooth-furred animals in flat light make dull B&W images.

REFLECTIONS_WILDLIFE.rationale: Animals at watering holes create stunning reflection compositions. f/5.6 keeps both animal and reflection reasonably sharp.
REFLECTIONS_WILDLIFE.proTip: Get your lens as close to the water surface as possible—even 1cm above creates the most dramatic reflection perspective.
REFLECTIONS_WILDLIFE.commonMistake: Shooting from eye-standing height eliminates the close reflection perspective. Get low to the ground.

NOCTURNAL_WILDLIFE.rationale: Owls, foxes, badgers, and other nocturnal animals require maximum ISO and fastest aperture. 1/125s freezes most nocturnal movement without needing flash, which would disturb animals.
NOCTURNAL_WILDLIFE.proTip: Use a red-light torch at low intensity — most nocturnal animals cannot clearly detect red wavelengths and are less likely to be startled. An IR spotlight with an appropriate sensor opens further possibilities.
NOCTURNAL_WILDLIFE.commonMistake: Using white light — this immediately stops natural behaviour and causes animals to flee. Red light at low intensity is the professional standard for nocturnal wildlife observation.

CAPTIVE_ZOO_ENCLOSURE.rationale: Wide aperture optically blurs enclosure bars and mesh fences. Fast shutter freezes unpredictable animal movement.
CAPTIVE_ZOO_ENCLOSURE.proTip: Hold the lens directly against the mesh or glass to eliminate it optically. At f/2.8, mesh just 5cm from the front element disappears completely into bokeh.
CAPTIVE_ZOO_ENCLOSURE.commonMistake: Shooting through glass at an angle — reflections destroy the shot. Position perpendicular to the glass and press a rubber lens hood against it to eliminate reflections.

SAFARI_MOVING_VEHICLE.rationale: Vehicle vibration and subject motion combine at safari speeds. 1/1000s eliminates both sources of blur and allows sharp shots from a moving vehicle.
SAFARI_MOVING_VEHICLE.proTip: Use a beanbag over the window sill as a lens support — far more stable than handholding from a vehicle. Switch to electronic shutter to eliminate mirror slap vibration.
SAFARI_MOVING_VEHICLE.commonMistake: Leaving IBIS/OIS active on a moving vehicle — stabilisation systems can misinterpret vehicle rocking as motion to compensate and actively introduce blur. Check your specific OIS mode.

HIDE_LONG_WAIT.rationale: Bird hides and wildlife blinds require pre-configured gear before the animal arrives. Pre-focused, pre-metered — no fumbling when the decisive moment happens.
HIDE_LONG_WAIT.proTip: Set your camera to anticipated conditions before dawn. Let eyes adjust to darkness before opening the hide window. Pre-focus on the expected perch or feeding station.
HIDE_LONG_WAIT.commonMistake: Leaving auto-ISO maximum range too high — in bright morning light auto-ISO may jump to ISO 6400 unnecessarily. Set auto-ISO max to 3200 for daylight hide work.
